skippedSources.name: McKinsey
skippedSources.reason: Email contained zero body content — teaser only with no sentences of analysis

items.title: Warsh Day 1: Market pricing 64% chance of a rate HIKE before year-end
items.idea: Kevin Warsh was sworn in as Fed Chair today — the man Trump nominated explicitly to cut rates. Bianco Research notes the market is now pricing a 64% probability of a rate HIKE before end of 2026. The gap between political mandate and market reality couldn't be more explicit on day one.
items.angle: This is the central tension of the next 12 months: Trump wants cuts, Warsh wants to look hawkish, and the data is forcing hikes. The June FOMC is the first real test.
items.tweetA: Warsh sworn in today. Trump picked him to cut rates. The market is pricing a 64% chance of a HIKE before year-end. Day one and the mandate is already underwater.
items.tweetB: The most interesting trade in macro right now: you're long a Fed chair whose political patron wants cuts, but the market is pricing hikes. Something has to give — either inflation cools fast, or this gets ugly by Q3.

items.title: Risk sentiment still neutral despite blistering equity rally — that's actually bullish
items.idea: TS Lombard's Daniel von Ahlen notes that despite the strong rally from March lows, the US Risk Sentiment Indicator remains neutral. The interpretation: many investors haven't actively participated, meaning the rally isn't stretched by optimistic positioning — which historically makes it more durable.
items.angle: Counter-intuitive framing: neutral sentiment after a big move = more runway, not danger. The bears waiting for a pullback to enter may be funding the next leg up.
items.tweetA: Everyone thinks the US equity rally is overextended. TS Lombard's risk sentiment indicator is still neutral. If most investors haven't participated — who's been buying? And what happens when they do?
items.tweetB: The rally from March lows hasn't been driven by extreme optimism. Sentiment is neutral. That's the thing about durable moves — they climb walls of worry, not waves of euphoria.

charts.desc: TS Lombard Risk Sentiment Indicator showing neutral reading despite equity rally

items.title: Cerebras IPO warning: 15 most-hyped tech IPOs since 2015 lost median 16.6% in 6 months
items.idea: Cerebras surged 68% on its first trading day Thursday. ProCap Insights data across 15 highly-anticipated tech IPOs since 2015 shows the median name lost 16.6% in the six months after its day-one rally. Cerebras already gave back 10% today. History says the pain is early and fast.
items.angle: Retail is buying the hype. The data says wait. Cerebras at 68% day-one means the bar is set; the clock is ticking on whether the business can meet it.
items.tweetA: Cerebras +68% day one. Then -10% day two. Data from 15 hyped tech IPOs since 2015: median lost 16.6% in the six months after the day-one pop. The enthusiasm is the setup, not the all-clear.
items.tweetB: Across 15 of the most hyped tech IPOs since 2015, the average investor who bought the day-one close was down 16.6% six months later. IPO day is when insiders sell. Retail buys the story. History is consistent.

charts.desc: 15 hyped tech IPOs vs 6-month post-IPO returns — powerful visual for Cerebras context

items.title: SPX overbought — but overbought alone has never been the bearish trigger
items.idea: Frank Cappelleri at CappNotes: 'Overbought conditions deserve respect, particularly after such a powerful run from the March lows. But overbought does not automatically mean bearish. Bearish price action occurs when bullish patterns start to fail; so far, that has yet to happen.' The distinction between being extended and turning is critical.
items.angle: Process matters: selling because RSI is high is different from selling because a pattern breaks. Until the latter happens, the correct read is consolidation risk — not reversal.
items.tweetA: SPX is overbought. But 'overbought' has never been a sell signal by itself. The signal is when bullish patterns start failing. That hasn't happened yet. Respecting the trend is not the same as ignoring the risk.
items.tweetB: The market has been overbought before. What kills rallies isn't the condition — it's the failure of the structure. Watch for the first lower low, not the RSI reading.

charts.desc: SPX with overbought indicator showing current stretched readings vs. historical instances

items.title: S&P 500 EPS estimates up 7% YTD — and 2027 estimates rose identically, ruling out pull-forward
items.idea: Sonu Varghese at Carson Group: S&P 500 2026 EPS expectations climbed from $308 to $331 (+7.5%) since start of year. Crucially, 2027 EPS expectations also rose 7% (from $355 to $380). If this were simple estimate pull-forward, 2027 would be flat or declining. The parallel move means analysts genuinely believe earnings power is expanding.
items.angle: The bull case has a quantitative spine. If the EPS revision cycle is real — not accounting tricks — then forward PE compression is happening in real time even at these prices.
items.tweetA: S&P 500 2026 EPS: $308 → $331 this year. You'd call it pull-forward if 2027 was flat. It isn't — 2027 rose 7% too, from $355 to $380. Analysts aren't just shuffling the deck. They're actually raising numbers.
items.tweetB: Bull markets that last are built on earnings growth, not multiple expansion. 2026 AND 2027 EPS estimates both up 7% YTD. The valuation argument gets harder to make when the E keeps moving up.

charts.desc: Dual chart showing 2026 and 2027 S&P 500 EPS estimate paths — parallel upward revision

items.title: Warsh confirmed 54-45 — most partisan Fed vote in modern history — inherits 3.8% CPI and 30yr at 5%
items.idea: Kevin Warsh was confirmed 54-45 — only one Democrat crossed over (Fetterman). He takes office with CPI at 3.8% (Iran war energy-driven), wholesale prices at 6% in April, the 30-year Treasury above 5%, and the most FOMC dissent (4 of 12 members) since 1992. The DOJ's building-renovation investigation into the Fed was dropped in April — clearing the path for confirmation.
items.angle: The geopolitical risk is now directly embedded in monetary policy. Iran war → energy → CPI → FOMC composition. The thread is connected.
items.tweetA: Warsh confirmed 54-45. Most partisan Fed vote ever. He walks into 3.8% CPI, 6% wholesale inflation, a 30-yr at 5%, and 4 FOMC dissenters. The market bought the news. The data hasn't cooperated yet.
items.tweetB: The DOJ investigation into Fed building renovations — widely seen as political pressure — was dropped in April. Warsh was confirmed weeks later. The independence question isn't hypothetical. It's operational.

items.title: Warsh's stealth rate-cut mechanism: switch Fed gauge from Core PCE (3.20%) to Trimmed Mean PCE (2.36%)
items.idea: Warsh has signaled he wants to replace Core PCE (excludes food/energy) with Trimmed Mean PCE (removes extreme movers each month) as the Fed's benchmark inflation gauge. Current readings: Core PCE 3.20%, Trimmed Mean PCE 2.36% — an 84bps gap. This requires no FOMC vote, just framework review. If it succeeds, the Fed is mathematically closer to declaring victory on inflation without actual improvement in the data.
items.angle: This is the most consequential variable in the Warsh era. If it happens, rate cuts become politically defensible even at 3%+ headline inflation. Watch for language in June FOMC statement.
items.tweetA: Warsh doesn't need to cut rates to engineer easier policy. He just needs to change the measuring stick. Core PCE: 3.20%. Trimmed Mean PCE: 2.36%. Same economy, 84bps cheaper. When the data doesn't cooperate — change the data.
items.tweetB: The Fed has 400+ PhD economists and can't out-forecast Bloomberg. Warsh's move: switch to an inflation metric that already reads near-target. No vote required. Just framework review. This is the rate-cut that doesn't look like a rate-cut.

items.title: Warsh may end post-meeting press conferences — FOMC days become structurally higher-vol events
items.idea: Warsh indicated he may not hold press conferences after every FOMC meeting, citing 'truth-seeking over repetition.' Under Powell, post-meeting press conferences were the primary mechanism for managing expectations — markets built positioning strategies around parsing Fed language. Removing that pipeline makes FOMC meeting days inherently more volatile and breaks the signal-extraction frameworks of systematic and AI quant funds.
items.angle: This is regime change for vol traders and systematic macro. The Fed communication premium in options expires with the press conference format.
items.tweetA: Warsh may not hold press conferences after every FOMC meeting. Think about what that means for the funds that spent years building AI models to parse Fed language. The signal disappears. FOMC days get wild.
items.tweetB: The Powell era created an entire industry around Fed communication analysis — every word, every pause, every shift from 'patient' to 'attentive.' Warsh is signaling he may just stop talking. You don't price that with existing vol models.

items.title: Historical Fed chair S&P returns: transitions are episodic noise — Volcker +219%, Greenspan +284%, Powell +183%
items.idea: Every Fed chair transition generates existential market narrative. The data: Volcker (raised rates to 20%) delivered +219.6% total equity return over tenure. Powell (pandemic + 525bps) delivered +183.2%. Greenspan (dot-com crash included) +284%. Bernanke (GFC) +35.8%. Yellen +58.6%. Since Volcker in 1979, S&P 500 total return: 3,840%+ through every crisis and transition.
items.angle: The contrarian here is staying invested. The noise machine says this transition is different. The historical record says it never is.
items.tweetA: Volcker raised rates to 20% and delivered +219% equity returns over his tenure. Powell survived a pandemic, 9% inflation, 525bps of hikes — still +183%. The Fed chair is not the variable that determines your wealth. The chair change is the story. The data is different.
items.tweetB: Since 1979: 5 Fed chairs, 3,840%+ S&P 500 return. Through recessions, crises, pandemics, political fights. The transitions feel enormous. They never are. Stay disciplined.

items.title: 30-yr Treasury at 5.12% — highest since 2007; hike probability 50% by year-end; cut probability <1%
items.idea: The 30-year Treasury closed at 5.12% Friday, highest since 2007. The 10-year at 4.60% and 2-year at 4.08% — both highest closes since February 2025. FedWatch now prices 50% probability of a rate hike by year-end; chance of any cut below 1%. RBC's Lori Calvasina flagged 5% on the 10-year as the level that 'spooks people' and compresses equity PE ratios.
items.angle: The 30-year at 5% is not neutral. Every long-duration asset — equities, real estate, corporate debt — is repricing. This is the dominant risk variable right now.
items.tweetA: 30-year Treasury: 5.12%. Highest since 2007. Hike probability by year-end: 50%. Cut probability: less than 1%. This is not the rate environment anyone priced in January. And equities are still near all-time highs.
items.tweetB: RBC's Lori Calvasina said 5% on the 10-year 'seems to spook people.' We're at 4.60% on the 10-year, 5.12% on the 30-year. The repricing has started. The equity market hasn't fully woken up yet.

items.title: SOX -4%; Nvidia -4.4%; AMD -5.7%; Micron -6.6%; Cerebras -10% after 68% first-day IPO surge
items.idea: Friday hit semiconductors hard: SOX -4.0%, Nvidia -4.4%, AMD -5.7%, Micron -6.6%, Intel -6%. Cerebras — +68% on Nasdaq debut Thursday — shed 10% on day two. The selloff was driven by bond yield pressure and profit-taking in high-flying tech, not company-specific news.
items.angle: Rising long rates = multiple compression for high-duration tech names. The playbook works until the rate environment breaks it. We may be at that inflection.
items.tweetA: SOX -4%. Nvidia -4.4%. AMD -5.7%. Micron -6.6%. Intel -6%. Cerebras -10% after +68% day one. The 30-year at 5.12% is doing what rising long rates always eventually do to high-multiple tech. It's not a surprise. It's the math.
items.tweetB: Cerebras: +68% Thursday, -10% Friday. The IPO hype window is now measured in hours, not weeks. When the rate backdrop shifts intraday, the story stocks feel it first and fastest.

items.title: April industrial production +0.68% m/m — more than 2x expectations, strongest since Feb 2025
items.idea: April industrial production came in at +0.68% m/m versus +0.3% expected — more than double consensus — boosted by autos, tech, and other industrial items. Strongest reading since February 2025. Lifted Q2 GDP estimates. This complicates the rate-cut case: the economy is growing faster than expected while inflation is sticky and yields are rising.
items.angle: Strong IP + sticky inflation + rising yields = the Fed's hands are tied. This is the data that makes 'hold through year-end' the base case.
items.tweetA: April industrial production: +0.68% m/m. Expectations were +0.3%. More than double. Strongest since February 2025. The economy is not slowing down. The Fed rate-cut story is over for 2026.
items.tweetB: Autos, tech, industrials all accelerated in April. IP more than doubled expectations. Q2 GDP estimates just moved higher. Warsh inherits an economy running hot, not one that needs stimulus. The political pressure for cuts meets an uncooperative dataset.

items.title: Trump China summit: zero Hormuz progress; Boeing order below expectations; market disappointed
items.idea: Trump's China trip produced no breakthrough on Hormuz or Iran. Trump explicitly said he did not push Xi to pressure Tehran. The Boeing order for 200 aircraft came in below expectations. Adam Crisafulli (Vital Knowledge) called the few headlines that emerged 'underwhelming.' Bond yields and crude prices continued rising through the session.
items.angle: The market had a residual Hormuz-deal hope embedded in prices. That hope is now explicitly off the table from Trump's own words. Oil stays bid.
items.tweetA: Trump visited China. Didn't ask Xi to pressure Iran. No Hormuz breakthrough. The Boeing order for 200 planes was below expectations. The summit was underwhelming by every measure. Oil up 2%+. Bond yields up. The geopolitical premium isn't going away.
items.tweetB: The assumption was China could broker an Iran deal. Trump said on record he didn't even raise it with Xi. If the diplomatic path isn't being pursued at the summit level — where is it? Crude at $110 with no resolution in sight.

items.title: Russell 2000 worst day since November; SPX 7-week streak survives by 0.1% — market breadth fracturing
items.idea: Russell 2000 fell -2.4% Friday (worst since November) while SPX managed +0.1% to extend its 7-week winning streak (longest since 2023). This divergence — mega-cap tech holding while small caps collapse — is the narrow leadership pattern that has characterized the entire rally. Keith Lerner (Truist): 'That broadening trade has really fizzled out... it's top heavy with tech.'
items.angle: Small caps are rate-sensitive by nature — rising yields hit them hardest. This is a stress signal for the breadth of the bull case.
items.tweetA: Russell 2000: worst session since November, -2.4%. S&P 500: +0.1%, 7-week streak intact. The index held. The market didn't. Small caps don't lie about what rising rates do to rate-sensitive companies.
items.tweetB: The broadening trade is dead. Tech mega-caps are holding the S&P 500 together while everything else deteriorates. That's the definition of fragile. 'It doesn't feel right to say that tech is going to lead forever' — Truist. Watch what happens when it doesn't.

items.title: Iraq-Iran terror plot: Kataib Hezbollah-linked national charged with Manhattan synagogue attack
items.idea: SDNY charged Mohammad Baqer Saad Dawood Al-Saadi, an Iraqi national, with funding or triggering attacks in the US including targeting a Manhattan synagogue — framed as Iran war retaliation. Suspect linked to Kataib Hezbollah, an Iran-aligned Iraqi militia. 18 attacks in Europe also alleged as planned or coordinated. Court appearance expected Wednesday.
items.angle: The Iran war is now generating domestic security escalation in the US. This expands the geopolitical risk premium — not just oil, but soft targets, security spending, and political pressure on Trump's Iran policy.
items.tweetA: An Iraqi national linked to Kataib Hezbollah — Iran's proxy militia — was charged with plotting a Manhattan synagogue attack. Framed explicitly as Iran war retaliation. 18 alleged European plots cited too. The conflict is no longer just a strait. It's in New York.
items.tweetB: The Iran war is now generating domestic terror charges in New York. Trump can't be seen as soft on Iran when US synagogues are being targeted. The diplomatic path just got harder, not easier.

items.title: Terror mechanics: 'capability injection' is the decisive variable — intent is abundant, access is the bottleneck
items.idea: The Pressure Point's analytical framework on the Iraq plot: 'Lone intent is abundant; the decisive variable is whether the suspect can acquire explosives, weapons, surveillance, transport, and dry-run time without tripping tripwires.' The FBI converts real-world attack pipelines into evidentiary ones via undercover operations. Target hardening creates displacement toward softer sites. Iran-aligned militias benefit from plausible deniability — they inspire or coordinate without clean command-and-control signatures.
items.angle: This framework applies globally — Iran-motivated individuals with intent is a large population; the constraint is operational capability. As the war extends, that bottleneck widens.
items.tweetA: The FBI's insight on domestic terror: intent is not the bottleneck. Capability is. Anyone can want to attack. The question is whether they can get explosives, transport, and timing without being intercepted. As the Iran war extends, that bottleneck widens.
items.tweetB: Iran-aligned militias benefit from plausible deniability — they can 'inspire' or 'coordinate' without leaving command-and-control signatures that meet attribution thresholds. You can arrest the actor. The network remains invisible.

items.title: Brent back to $110/bbl — US-Iran talks stalled on nuclear bottleneck; China summit yielded nothing
items.idea: Brent crude rose ~$8/bbl to just under $110/bbl this week as optimism for a deal faded. US-Iran talks are thoroughly stalled on the nuclear bottleneck (Iran's enrichment program is the specific impasse). Trump didn't raise Hormuz with Xi. Term structure is firming — timespreads bottomed Monday and grinded higher all week, with the back of the curve continuing to rerate as global inventories are depleted.
items.angle: The market tried to price in a deal premium, then had to reverse it. Each failed breakthrough is bullish crude. The structural case (inventory depletion + term structure) reinforces the geopolitical case.
items.tweetA: Brent back to $110/bbl. US-Iran nuclear talks stalled. Trump didn't raise Hormuz with Xi. The back of the crude curve keeps rerate-ing higher as global inventories deplete. The bull case for oil doesn't need new catalysts — it just needs nothing to go right.
items.tweetB: Oil timespreads: bottomed Monday, grinded higher all week. The back of the curve is repricing upward as inventories are drawn down globally. This isn't speculative flow — hot money is actually selling. The physical market is doing the work.

charts.desc: Brent timespread evolution showing bottoming and grind higher — physical vs. financial market divergence

items.title: NY Harbor gasoline cracks approaching 2022 all-time highs — US gasoline market structurally tight
items.idea: New York Harbor gasoline crack spreads are rising toward the all-time high set during the 2022 energy crisis summer. US gasoline stocks are drawing fast. ARA European stocks at precarious seasonal lows. This is independent confirmation that crude supply disruption is transmitting into refined product scarcity.
items.angle: If gasoline cracks hit all-time highs, that's a retail inflation shock. The Fed cannot cut rates when gasoline is at record margins. This connects Iran war → oil → gasoline → CPI → FOMC.
items.tweetA: NY Harbor gasoline crack spreads are approaching the 2022 all-time highs. That was the energy crisis summer. We're not there yet — but the direction is clear. The Iran war isn't just a crude story. It's a pump-price story. And that kills rate cuts.
items.tweetB: US gasoline inventories drawing fast. ARA European stocks at precarious seasonal lows. Crack spreads approaching all-time highs. This is what Hormuz disruption looks like six months in — not in the crude price, but in the refined product margin.

charts.desc: NY Harbor gasoline crack spreads vs. 2022 all-time high — striking proximity

items.title: Hot money sold crude at steepest rate since war began — but speculative flows now irrelevant to price
items.idea: Market positioning data confirmed the steepest week of proportional speculative selling of major crude contracts since the Iran war began. Despite this, crude prices rose ~$8/bbl. The author's conclusion: 'hot money flows have had an increasingly inconsequential effect on the crude pricing complex compared to their typically outsized role in non-crisis markets.' The physical market has taken over price discovery from the paper market.
items.angle: This is a regime change in crude price formation. CTA positioning signals are now noise. The physical reality is the only signal that matters.
items.tweetA: Speculators sold crude at the fastest rate since the Iran war started. Prices went up anyway. When the physical market is this tight, financial flows don't matter. The paper market has lost control of price discovery.
items.tweetB: In normal oil markets, CTA/speculative positioning predicts price direction. That relationship is broken. The steepest week of speculative selling since the war started — and crude rallied $8. Physical reality is overriding the financial layer.

charts.desc: Positioning data showing speculative selling vs. price action divergence

items.title: UAE doubling Hormuz bypass capacity; Canada advancing West Coast pipeline; US SPR at all-time outflow
items.idea: Three structural developments: (1) UAE is doubling its pipeline capacity to route crude around the Strait of Hormuz. (2) Ottawa and Alberta are advancing a West Coast pipeline plan, creating a new Canadian export route. (3) US SPR release hit an all-time high outflow rate. Separately, Iranian onshore inventories are rising as tanker loadings collapse — Iran is storing crude it cannot export.
items.angle: Bypass infrastructure takes 12-18 months to matter at scale. Iranian storage buildup is a leading indicator of future supply release if a deal is reached — the coiled spring is visible.
items.tweetA: UAE is doubling its Hormuz bypass pipeline capacity. Canada is building a new West Coast oil export route. US SPR outflows at all-time highs. None of these fix the problem today — but they tell you exactly what the world thinks about the next two years.
items.tweetB: Iranian onshore oil inventories rising while tanker loadings collapse. Iran is storing crude it can't sell. That's future supply waiting for a deal. If Hormuz opens, the price reversal will be fast and violent. The coiled spring is visible.

items.title: Servers = 60% of 1GW data center TCO: $5B of $8.5B annual cost — energy is not the bottleneck
items.idea: GovAI/Epoch researchers modeled the full costs of a typical one-gigawatt US AI data center. Servers alone account for $5B of the $8.5B annual total cost of ownership — 60% of TCO. Energy and all other opex make up the remaining $3.5B. This inverts the common narrative that power is the binding constraint in AI infrastructure.
items.angle: The investment thesis follows the cost: if servers are 60% of TCO, the supply chain that matters is compute hardware, not power generation. Follow the chip, not the grid.
items.tweetA: 1 gigawatt AI data center: $8.5B annual total cost. Servers alone: $5B (60%). Energy and everything else: $3.5B. The narrative is 'we need more power.' The data says we need more chips. Follow the cost, not the story.
items.tweetB: Everyone is building power plants for AI. The math says servers are 60% of total data center cost — 3x more than energy. The power narrative is real but overcrowded. The server/chip supply chain is where the money actually lives.

charts.desc: Data center TCO breakdown — servers 60% vs. energy and other costs. Clean, tweetable.

items.title: Claude overperforms on SWE benchmarks, underperforms on math — capability profile is domain-specific
items.idea: Epoch AI's Alexander Barry, using the Domain-specific ECI Explorer, found that Claude models systematically overperform on software engineering benchmarks and underperform on math benchmarks relative to their general Epoch Capabilities Index scores. Benchmark averages obscure domain-level divergence in models with strong specializations.
items.angle: Relevant for anyone routing tasks to models: Claude for code generation, different models for quantitative/math tasks. Also relevant for safety — benchmark averages can hide meaningful capability gaps.
items.tweetA: Epoch AI ran domain-specific capability analysis on Claude. Result: overperforms on software engineering, underperforms on math. The 'general capability' benchmark average is masking real domain divergence. The model you pick for coding is not the same model for quant.
items.tweetB: Claude's capability profile is not symmetric. Strong in software. Weak in math. If you're routing AI tasks by model, this matters. If you're evaluating models by average benchmark scores, you're missing the shape of the capability curve.

items.title: AI superstar researchers earn 10-100x peers — winner-take-most economics at marginal capability differences
items.idea: Epoch AI's Anson Ho argues that enormous pay disparities among AI researchers — where superstars earn 10-100x colleagues — emerge not from proportional quality differences but from winner-take-most tournament economics. Even a researcher marginally better at a critical bottleneck problem captures enormous value because the payoff of breakthrough is binary. The gap is structural and likely to grow.
items.angle: The AI talent market as an asset class. The compensation arms race is not slowing — it's accelerating. Companies paying $100M packages are rationally pricing option value on the next paradigm shift.
items.tweetA: Top AI researchers earn 10-100x what colleagues make. Epoch AI's analysis: this isn't because they're 10-100x better. It's because being marginally better at a critical bottleneck in a winner-take-all race has binary payoffs. The marginal researcher gets the breakthrough. The other doesn't.
items.tweetB: Meta is cutting 8,000 employees while paying AI researchers $100M. That's not cognitive dissonance — that's rational tournament economics. The superstar effect in AI is larger than in any previous knowledge economy. The value of being first compounds.

items.title: Epoch flagged fatal errors in ~33% of FrontierMath benchmark problems — AI evaluation reliability at risk
items.idea: Epoch AI is conducting an AI-assisted review of FrontierMath Tiers 1-4 and has flagged fatal errors in approximately one-third of problems — though they believe most flagged problems are valid and will release updated scores after human review. Broader question: if the leading math benchmark for frontier AI has a 33% preliminary error rate, how reliable is the broader ecosystem of AI capability evaluations?
items.angle: Benchmark quality is the silent risk in AI progress claims. If the measuring stick is broken, capability comparisons lose meaning.
items.tweetA: Epoch AI ran an AI-assisted review of FrontierMath — the leading math benchmark for frontier AI. Flagged fatal errors in ~33% of problems. They think most are valid. But the question stands: if the benchmark has a 33% error rate, what are we actually measuring?
items.tweetB: The AI capability race is benchmarked. The benchmarks are being benchmarked. FrontierMath — ~33% of problems flagged for fatal errors on preliminary review. This doesn't mean the models are weaker than we thought. It means we might not know how strong they are.

items.title: Meta: 8,000 layoffs despite record profits; raises cut while AI researchers get $100M packages
items.idea: Meta announced 8,000 layoffs this month despite record revenue and profits. Zuckerberg told employees cuts are 'not related to AI.' But simultaneously: annual raises cut 5% this year (10% prior year) while Meta recruits AI researchers with packages reportedly reaching $100 million. The two policies running in parallel are generating obvious dissonance for non-executive employees.
items.angle: This is the corporate AI transition in real time: capital concentrating into a tiny class of AI researchers while the broader workforce shrinks. It's not unique to Meta — it's the industry playbook.
items.tweetA: Meta: record profits. Record revenue. 8,000 layoffs. Annual raises cut 5% (10% the year before). AI researchers hired at $100M packages. Zuckerberg says the layoffs 'aren't about AI.' The employees watching this happen have a different read.
items.tweetB: The AI productivity trade-off is no longer theoretical. Meta is running the experiment live: shrink the workforce, concentrate capital into elite AI talent, maintain profitability. The math works for shareholders. The culture breaks for everyone else.

items.title: TSMC predicts semiconductor market reaches $1.5 trillion by 2030
items.idea: TSMC has published a forecast that the global semiconductor market will reach $1.5 trillion by 2030. The current market is approximately $600-650 billion — a 2.3x expansion in roughly four years. TSMC has the best demand visibility of any company in the world — they see every major chip order before it ships. When they forecast $1.5T, that is not marketing. It is order backlog.
items.angle: This foundational data point supports sustained AI infrastructure investment for years, not quarters. The AI capex cycle is not a 2025-2026 event.
items.tweetA: TSMC forecasts the global semiconductor market hits $1.5 trillion by 2030. That's from ~$650B today — a 2.3x expansion in four years. TSMC sees every chip order before anyone else. This isn't a bullish forecast. It's a backlog.
items.tweetB: The semiconductor market at $1.5T by 2030 implies AI buildout sustains for years, not quarters. TSMC doesn't publish market forecasts casually. They have the order book. The AI capex cycle is not a 2025-2026 event.

items.title: Powell's tenure ends: M2 +42% in 22 months, 525bps in 16 months — a record debated for a generation
items.idea: Jerome Powell's tenure as Fed Chair ended Friday. The statistical record: M2 grew $6.4 trillion in 22 months (42% expansion — larger than WWII response and 2008 response). The Fed held rates at zero until March 2022 when CPI was already at 7.9% and had been above target for over a year. Then 525 basis points in 16 months, most aggressive since Volcker. Warsh inherits a balance sheet 51.8% larger than when Powell started.
items.angle: The deferred consequences of the Powell era are Warsh's problem. The balance sheet, the inflation expectations, the credibility gap — all inherited.
items.tweetA: Powell is gone. The record: $6.4T in M2 creation in 22 months (42% expansion). Called it transitory. Didn't hike until CPI hit 7.9%. Then 525bps in 16 months. The sequence was print, deny, slam the brakes. Warsh inherits the balance sheet — $9 trillion and counting.
items.tweetB: The Powell era: largest M2 expansion in American history, biggest inflation miss since the 1970s, most aggressive hike cycle since Volcker — all in eight years. The Fed's credibility took the hit. Warsh now owns the residual.
